question:
Problem B-2. Find the minimum value of
\[
(u-v)^{2}+\left(\sqrt{2-u^{2}}-\frac{9}{v}\right)^{2}
\]
for \( 0<u<\sqrt{2} \) and \( v>0 \).
solution:
B-2.
The problem asks for the minimum distance between the quarter of the circle \( x^{2}+y^{2}=2 \) in the open first quadrant and the half of the hyperbola \( x y=9 \) in that quadrant. Since the tangents to the respective curves at \( (1,1) \) and \( (3,3) \) separate the curves and are both perpendicular to \( x=y \), the minimum distance is 8 .

descriptive_long.question:
Problem B-2. Find the minimum value of
\[
(firstvar-secondvar)^{2}+\left(\sqrt{2-firstvar^{2}}-\frac{9}{secondvar}\right)^{2}
\]
for \( 0<firstvar<\sqrt{2} \) and \( secondvar>0 \).
descriptive_long.solution:
B-2.
The problem asks for the minimum distance between the quarter of the circle \( axisvalue^{2}+ordinate^{2}=2 \) in the open first quadrant and the half of the hyperbola \( axisvalue\,ordinate=9 \) in that quadrant. Since the tangents to the respective curves at \( (1,1) \) and \( (3,3) \) separate the curves and are both perpendicular to \( axisvalue=ordinate \), the minimum distance is 8 .

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Problem B-2. Find the minimum value of
\[
( dragonfly - honeycomb )^{2}+\left(\sqrt{2-dragonfly^{2}}-\frac{9}{ honeycomb }\right)^{2}
\]
for \( 0< dragonfly <\sqrt{2} \) and \( honeycomb >0 \).
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
B-2.
The problem asks for the minimum distance between the quarter of the circle \( tangerine^{2}+porcupine^{2}=2 \) in the open first quadrant and the half of the hyperbola \( tangerine\,porcupine=9 \) in that quadrant. Since the tangents to the respective curves at \( (1,1) \) and \( (3,3) \) separate the curves and are both perpendicular to \( tangerine=porcupine \), the minimum distance is 8 .

B-2.
The problem asks for the minimum distance between the quarter of the circle \( anchorednum^{2}+steadycount^{2}=2 \) in the open first quadrant and the half of the hyperbola \( anchorednum steadycount=9 \) in that quadrant. Since the tangents to the respective curves at \( (1,1) \) and \( (3,3) \) separate the curves and are both perpendicular to \( anchorednum=steadycount \), the minimum distance is 8 .

B-2.
The problem asks for the minimum distance between the quarter of the circle \( nfqzmpwy^{2}+ksrnvxje^{2}=2 \) in the open first quadrant and the half of the hyperbola \( nfqzmpwy\,ksrnvxje=9 \) in that quadrant. Since the tangents to the respective curves at \( (1,1) \) and \( (3,3) \) separate the curves and are both perpendicular to \( nfqzmpwy=ksrnvxje \), the minimum distance is 8 .

kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout we write  
\[
y=(s,s,s,t,t,t),\qquad s>t>0,\quad st=9
\;\Longrightarrow\;
t=\frac{9}{s},\;s>3.
\tag{0}
\]

1.  Reformulation of the inner problem  
Fix \(y\in H\) and abbreviate  
\[
\Phi(y):=\min_{x\in S}D(x,y).
\]
For any \(x\in S\),
\[
D(x,y)=\sum_{i=1}^{6}x_{i}^{2}+3s^{2}+3t^{2}-2\bigl(s(x_{1}+x_{2}+x_{3})+t(x_{4}+x_{5}+x_{6})\bigr)
      =30+C(s)-2\langle x,y\rangle,
\]
where \(C(s)=3s^{2}+3t^{2}\) is independent of \(x\).  
Hence
\[
\Phi(y)=30+C(s)-2\max_{x\in S}\langle x,y\rangle.
\tag{1.1}
\]
Minimising the distance is therefore equivalent to \emph{maximising} the
linear form \(\langle x,y\rangle\) over \(S\).

2.  A sharp upper bound for the inner product  
Write
\[
a:=x_{1}+x_{2}+x_{3},\quad b:=x_{4}+x_{5}+x_{6}=12-a
\qquad(0\le a,b\le 12).
\]
Let
\(
\sigma_{1}:=x_{1}^{2}+x_{2}^{2}+x_{3}^{2},\;
\sigma_{2}:=x_{4}^{2}+x_{5}^{2}+x_{6}^{2}=30-\sigma_{1}.
\)
By Cauchy-Schwarz,
\[
\sigma_{1}\ge\frac{a^{2}}{3},\qquad \sigma_{2}\ge\frac{b^{2}}{3},
\]
and therefore
\[
\frac{a^{2}}{3}+\frac{b^{2}}{3}\le30
\;\Longrightarrow\;
(a-6)^{2}\le9
\;\Longrightarrow\;
3\le a\le9.
\tag{2.1}
\]

Because \(s>t\), we estimate
\[
\langle x,y\rangle
  =sa+tb
  =12t+(s-t)a
  \le12t+(s-t)\cdot 9,
\tag{2.2}
\]
and equality in (2.2) requires simultaneously
\[
\text{(i) } a=9
\quad\text{and}\quad
\text{(ii) } \sigma_{1}=\frac{a^{2}}{3},\; \sigma_{2}=\frac{b^{2}}{3},
\]
i.e.  
\[
x_{1}=x_{2}=x_{3}=3,\qquad
x_{4}=x_{5}=x_{6}=1.
\tag{2.3}
\]
Thus any maximiser of \(\langle x,y\rangle\) must be the point
\[
x^{\star}:=(3,3,3,1,1,1),
\tag{2.4}
\]
together with its images under the group
\(\mathfrak S_{3}\times\mathfrak S_{3}\) that separately permutes the
first and the last three coordinates.  (Permuting a ``\(3\)'' into a
\(t\)-slot or a ``\(1\)'' into an \(s\)-slot would lower \(a\) below \(9\)
and hence strictly decrease the inner product.)

\emph{Consequently \(x^{\star}\) is the \underline{unique} minimiser of
\(x\mapsto D(x,y)\).  In particular, every optimal \(x\) satisfies
\(x_{i}>0\); the gap in the original argument is now closed.}

Evaluating (1.1) at \(x^{\star}\) gives the reduced one-variable
objective
\[
\boxed{\;
\Phi(y)=D(x^{\star},y)=
3\Bigl[(s-3)^{2}+\Bigl(\tfrac{9}{s}-1\Bigr)^{2}\Bigr]
=:F(s)},\qquad s>3.
\tag{2.5}
\]

3.  Strict convexity of \(F\)  
Set
\(
f(s)=(s-3)^{2}+(\tfrac{9}{s}-1)^{2},\;
F=3f.
\)
Routine differentiation yields  
\[
f'(s)=\frac{2\bigl(s^{4}-3s^{3}+9s-81\bigr)}{s^{3}},\qquad
f''(s)=2+\frac{18}{s^{3}}+\frac{54(9-s)}{s^{4}}>0\quad(s>3),
\]
so \(F\) is strictly convex on \((3,\infty)\).  Hence it admits exactly
one minimiser \(s_{0}>3\), determined by the quartic equation
\[
p(s):=s^{4}-3s^{3}+9s-81=0,\qquad p'(s)>0\;(s>3).
\tag{3.1}
\]

4.  Closed radicals for \(s_{0}\) (Ferrari-Cardano)  
Depressing \(p\) with \(s=z+\tfrac34\) gives  
\[
z^{4}-\frac{27}{8}z^{2}+\frac{45}{8}z-\frac{19\,251}{256}=0.
\]
Ferrari's method reduces this to a resolvent cubic whose unique real
root is
\[
y_{0}=
-\frac{27}{48}
+\sqrt[3]{\frac{11\,583}{4\,096}
          +\mathrm i
           \frac{\sqrt{254\,223\,895\,167}}{4\,096}}
+\sqrt[3]{\frac{11\,583}{4\,096}
          -\mathrm i
           \frac{\sqrt{254\,223\,895\,167}}{4\,096}}.
\]
Put  
\[
U=\sqrt{\,2y_{0}+\tfrac{27}{8}},\qquad
V=\sqrt{-2y_{0}-\tfrac{27}{8}+\tfrac{45}{4U}},
\]
then  
\[
\boxed{\;
s_{0}=\frac34+\frac{U+V}{2}},\qquad t_{0}=\frac{9}{s_{0}}.
}
\tag{4.1}
\]
All radicals are over \(\mathbb Q\), as requested.  Numerically  
\(
s_{0}\approx3.829016065,\;
t_{0}\approx2.351538472.
\)

5.  The minimum value  
Substituting \(s_{0}\) into (2.5) gives  
\[
\boxed{\;
D_{\min}=3\Bigl[(s_{0}-3)^{2}+\Bigl(\tfrac{9}{s_{0}}-1\Bigr)^{2}\Bigr]
      =\frac{3}{s_{0}^{2}}
        \bigl(s_{0}^{4}-6s_{0}^{3}+10s_{0}^{2}-18s_{0}+81\bigr).
}
\tag{5.1}
\]
Eliminating \(s_{0}^{4}\) via \(p(s_{0})=0\) one may also write the
numerator as \(-3s_{0}^{3}+10s_{0}^{2}-27s_{0}+162\).
Numerically \(D_{\min}\approx7.541970271\).

6.  Global minimisers  
(i)  The inner problem has the unique minimiser \(x^{\star}\) in (2.4).  
(ii) The outer function \(F\) is strictly convex with unique minimiser
     \(s_{0}\).

Hence the \emph{only} pair attaining the global minimum is
\[
\boxed{\;
x^{\star}=(3,3,3,1,1,1),\qquad
y^{\star}=(s_{0},s_{0},s_{0},t_{0},t_{0},t_{0}).
}
\tag{6.1}
\]

7.  Proof of Part (A)  
For each fixed \(y\in H\) the set \(S\) is compact and
\(x\mapsto D(x,y)\) is continuous, so \(\Phi(y)\) is attained.
Writing \(y\) as in (0) identifies \(H\) with the open ray \((3,\infty)\);
the map \(s\mapsto F(s)\) of (2.5) is continuous, satisfies
\(
\lim_{s\downarrow3}F(s)=12,\;
\lim_{s\to\infty}F(s)=\infty,
\)
and is strictly convex.  Therefore it attains its minimum exactly at
\(s_{0}\), and the pair \((x^{\star},y^{\star})\) in (6.1) is the sole
global minimiser, completing the proof.  
\(\square\)

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1. Higher dimensionality: the problem lives in ℝ⁶ rather than ℝ²,
   introducing six variables instead of two.
2. Multiple interacting constraints: the feasible set S is the
   intersection of a positive orthant with both a hyperplane
   (Σxᵢ = 12) and a sphere (Σxᵢ² = 30); H is a 1–dimensional manifold
   defined simultaneously by an ordering condition (s>t) and a
   high–degree algebraic constraint (s³t³ = 9³).
3. Sophisticated optimisation: one must use Lagrange multipliers to
   locate the nearest point of S to a given y, revealing an
   internal two–point structure {1,3}.
4. Reduction to a quartic: the remaining single–variable problem
   leads to the non-trivial quartic equation s⁴ – 3s³ + 9s – 81 = 0,
   whose root cannot be guessed and must be solved with Ferrari’s
   method or an equivalent algebraic apparatus.
5. Exact closed form: the final answer demands radicals of nested
   radicals (formula (11)), far beyond the elementary arithmetic
   needed for the original 2-dimensional problem.
6. Subtle case distinction: one has to decide which block of
   coordinates should be matched with which value of x,
   a point that disappears entirely in the original statement.

Altogether the enhanced variant compels the solver to combine
multivariate calculus, symmetric-constraint reasoning, and full
quartic algebra, making it substantially harder than both the
original Putnam problem and the current kernel variant.

original_kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout write  
\[
y=(s,s,s,t,t,t)\qquad\bigl(s>t>0,\;st=9\bigr).
\tag{0}
\]

\textbf{1.  Inner minimisation: \(\displaystyle \Phi(y)=\min_{x\in S}D(x,y)\).}

Minimise \(x\mapsto D(x,y)\) on \(S\) subject to the constraints  
\[
g_{1}(x)=\sum_{i=1}^{6}x_{i}-12=0,\qquad
g_{2}(x)=\sum_{i=1}^{6}x_{i}^{2}-30=0.
\]
Introduce Lagrange multipliers \(\lambda,\mu\) and set  
\[
L(x,\lambda,\mu)=D(x,y)+\lambda g_{1}+\mu g_{2}.
\]
Stationarity yields, for every \(i\),  
\[
2(x_{i}-y_{i})+\lambda+2\mu x_{i}=0
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
x_{i}= \frac{y_{i}-\lambda/2}{1+\mu},
\tag{1.1}
\]
provided \(1+\mu\neq 0\).  (The case \(1+\mu=0\) leads to
a contradiction with the two quadratic constraints and is ruled out.)

Because the six coordinates of \(y\) take only the two values
\(s\) and \(t\), every critical vector \(x\) produced by (1.1)
assumes at most two distinct values.  By symmetry we may write  
\[
x=(\alpha,\alpha,\alpha,\beta,\beta,\beta)\qquad\bigl(\alpha,\beta>0\bigr).
\tag{1.2}
\]
Substituting (1.2) in the constraints gives the linear-quadratic system  
\[
\alpha+\beta=4,\qquad
\alpha^{2}+\beta^{2}=10,
\]
whence \(\alpha\beta=3\) and therefore  
\[
\{\alpha,\beta\}=\{1,3\}.
\]
Thus \emph{two} stationary points are obtained:
\[
x_{A}=(3,3,3,1,1,1),\qquad
x_{B}=(1,1,1,3,3,3).
\tag{1.3}
\]

\textbf{2.  Selecting the true minimiser for fixed \(y\).}

Compute the difference
\[
\begin{aligned}
D(x_{B},y)-D(x_{A},y)
   &=\sum_{i=1}^{6}\bigl( (x_{B,i}-y_{i})^{2}-(x_{A,i}-y_{i})^{2}\bigr) \\
   &=3\bigl[(1-s)^{2}-(3-s)^{2}+(3-t)^{2}-(1-t)^{2}\bigr] \\
   &=12\,(s-t)\;>\;0.
\end{aligned}
\]
Hence \(x_{A}\) gives the smaller distance for every admissible
\((s,t)\), so the inner minimum is uniquely attained at  
\[
x^{\ast}=x_{A}=(3,3,3,1,1,1).
\tag{2.1}
\]
Therefore
\[
\boxed{\;
\Phi(y)\;=\;D(x^{\ast},y)
         \;=\;3\Bigl[(s-3)^{2}+\Bigl(\tfrac{9}{s}-1\Bigr)^{2}\Bigr]
         =:F(s)
\;},
\qquad s>3.
\tag{2.2}
\]

\textbf{3.  Outer minimisation: \(\displaystyle D_{\min}=\min_{s>3}F(s).\)}

Define
\[
f(s)=(s-3)^{2}+\Bigl(\tfrac{9}{s}-1\Bigr)^{2},\qquad s>3,
\quad\text{so that }F(s)=3f(s).
\]

\emph{Convexity.}  Differentiating,
\[
f'(s)=2(s-3)-\frac{18(9-s)}{s^{3}}
      =\frac{2\bigl(s^{4}-3s^{3}+9s-81\bigr)}{s^{3}},
\tag{3.1}
\]
\[
f''(s)=2+\frac{18}{s^{3}}+\frac{54(9-s)}{s^{4}}>0\quad(s>3),
\]
so \(f\) (hence \(F\)) is strictly convex on \((3,\infty)\).
Consequently there exists a unique minimiser \(s_{0}>3\), determined
by the quartic equation coming from \(f'(s)=0\):
\[
s_{0}^{4}-3s_{0}^{3}+9s_{0}-81=0.
\tag{3.2}
\]

\textbf{4.  Exact solution of the quartic.}

Put \(s=z+\dfrac34\) to eliminate the cubic term.  Ferrari's method
reduces (3.2) to the depressed quartic
\[
z^{4}+pz^{2}+qz+r=0,\qquad
p=-\frac{27}{8},\;q=\frac{45}{8},\;r=-\frac{19251}{256}.
\tag{4.1}
\]

Introduce the resolvent cubic
\[
y^{3}-\frac{p}{2}y^{2}-ry+\Bigl(\frac{rp}{2}-\frac{q^{2}}{8}\Bigr)=0.
\]
Clearing denominators,
\[
y^{3}+\frac{9}{16}y^{2}+\frac{19251}{256}y+\frac{1075275}{4096}=0.
\tag{4.2}
\]

Cardano's formula gives the unique real root
\[
y_{0}=\sqrt[3]{-\frac{81}{2}+\frac{\sqrt{1\,075\,275}}{8}}
      +\sqrt[3]{-\frac{81}{2}-\frac{\sqrt{1\,075\,275}}{8}}
      -\frac{9}{16}.
\]

Set  
\[
U=\sqrt{\,2y_{0}+\frac{27}{8}},\qquad
V=\sqrt{-2y_{0}-\frac{27}{8}+\frac{45}{4U}}.
\]
Among the four resulting roots \(z=\pm\dfrac{U\pm V}{2}\) only one
yields \(s=z+\dfrac34>3\).  Define
\[
\boxed{\;
s_{0}=\frac34+\frac{U+V}{2}},\qquad
\boxed{\;
t_{0}=\frac{9}{s_{0}}}.
\tag{4.3}
\]
(Numerically \(s_{0}\approx3.832526901\), \(t_{0}\approx2.348389020\).)

\textbf{5.  Minimum value.}

Using (2.2) and (4.3),
\[
\boxed{\;
D_{\min}=3\Bigl[(s_{0}-3)^{2}+\Bigl(\tfrac{9}{s_{0}}-1\Bigr)^{2}\Bigr]\;}.
\tag{5.1}
\]
Numerically \(D_{\min}\approx7.526960797\).

\textbf{6.  All minimising pairs.}

For each \(y\in H\) the inner minimisation is uniquely solved by
\(x^{\ast}=x_{A}\) (Step 2), while the outer problem has the unique
minimiser \(s=s_{0}\) (strict convexity in Step 3).  Hence the only
pair attaining \(D_{\min}\) is  
\[
\boxed{\;
x^{\ast}=(3,3,3,1,1,1),\qquad
y^{\ast}=(s_{0},s_{0},s_{0},t_{0},t_{0},t_{0})\;}.
\tag{6.1}
\]

\textbf{7.  Existence of the global minimum (Part A).}

The set \(S\) is compact (closed and bounded).  For every fixed
\(y\in H\) the map \(x\mapsto D(x,y)\) is continuous, hence attains a
minimum on \(S\); so \(\Phi:H\to\mathbb R\) defined by \(\Phi(y)=\min_{x\in S}D(x,y)\)
is well defined.  Formula (2.2) shows that
\(\Phi(y)=F(s)\to\infty\) as \(s\to\infty\) and
\(\Phi(y)\to12\) as \(s\downarrow 3\).  Therefore
\(\Phi\) attains its global minimum at \(s=s_{0}\).
Since \(H\) is continuous in \(s\) and the inner minimiser
\(x^{\ast}\) depends continuously on \(y\), the pair
\((x^{\ast},y^{\ast})\) realises \(D_{\min}\), completing Part (A).

\hfill\(\blacksquare\)



------------------------------------------------------------

   introducing six variables instead of two.
2. Multiple interacting constraints: the feasible set S is the
   intersection of a positive orthant with both a hyperplane

   high–degree algebraic constraint (s³t³ = 9³).

   whose root cannot be guessed and must be solved with Ferrari’s
